features.summary: Recent updates in the compositing and layers area of Firefox have introduced several optimizations aimed at improving performance and responsiveness for users. Significant changes include a **stronger focus on handling touch events more efficiently**, particularly during fast scrolling actions, which means smoother interactions when navigating websites. Additionally, the updates optimize how images are managed in the layer system, ensuring that references to textures are handled more reliably, enhancing the overall stability and quality of visuals. Together, these improvements contribute to a more responsive and visually appealing browsing experience, particularly for content that relies on touch or involves complex graphical elements.

features.summary: Recent updates to Firefox's WebRender, the GPU compositor that enhances rendering performance for graphics, have focused on optimizing how visual elements are handled on the screen. One key improvement is the **addition of support for advanced image manipulation through quad shaders**, allowing for more efficient image cutouts and rendering. This means that images will be processed more effectively, improving overall visual quality and performance. Additionally, adjustments have been made to how offsets and clipping work in the rendering process, which helps ensure that scrolling and visual elements are accurately positioned. These changes collectively aim to make web graphics smoother and more responsive, directly benefiting user experience during visually rich interactions like watching videos or playing games online.

features.summary: Recent updates to the 2D graphics component of Firefox focus on enhancing font handling and optimizing performance. The browser now ensures that the default font family is properly initialized before usage, which helps avoid issues with displaying text. Additionally, there are improvements to the efficiency of processing layout metrics in text frames, which can lead to smoother performance for rendering web pages. **Moreover, the allowed features for video processing have been refined, shifting to a blocklist approach for unsupported features, ensuring that only compatible elements are utilized, which ultimately aims to provide a more reliable video playback experience.** These changes collectively contribute to better user experience and stability, particularly in text rendering and media handling.

features.summary: Recent updates to the Canvas area of Firefox focus on enhancing the performance and compatibility of WebGL, which is the technology used for rendering interactive 3D graphics in web browsers. The changes include improving the maximum number of color draw buffers that can be used and ensuring that WebGL contexts adhere to modern standards by requiring certain capabilities like `GL_SHADING_LANGUAGE_VERSION >= 150`. **These updates are crucial for developers as they promote better graphics rendering and reduce compatibility issues, ultimately leading to a smoother and more visually appealing experience for users.** Additionally, the removal of compatibility profile contexts streamlines how WebGL operates, allowing for more efficient graphics processing.

features.summary: Recent updates to the 2D graphics library, known as Moz2D, have improved memory management by implementing more efficient allocation methods. By using **MakeUnique** and **MakeRefPtr**, the library can now handle pointers more effectively, resulting in better performance and potentially lower memory usage when creating graphic objects. Additionally, adopting modern programming techniques like using _for_each_ for iterations enhances code readability and maintainability. These changes, while not immediately visible to users, contribute to the overall robustness and efficiency of the Firefox browser's graphics rendering capabilities.

features.label: OpenGL / GL contexts

features.summary: Recent updates to the OpenGL implementation in Firefox focus on optimizing how graphics functions are loaded. Specifically, the changes ensure that certain non-core functions, like `glGetGraphicsResetStatus` and KHR_debug symbols, are not loaded unless absolutely necessary. This helps streamline the resource usage of the browser by avoiding unnecessary loading of graphics functions that may not be used. These improvements contribute to better performance and efficiency, especially for users running graphics-intensive applications or websites. **This means that Firefox can deliver a smoother experience when handling complex graphics, which is increasingly important as web content becomes more advanced.**

features.label: Image decoding

patches.author: Tyler B. Thrailkill
patches.subject: Add link-parameter parsing and rendering for svg

features.summary: Recent updates to Firefox's image decoding capabilities have introduced improved support for SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) files by adding **link-parameter parsing and rendering**. This enhancement allows for more accurate and flexible handling of links embedded within SVG images, enabling developers to create richer, interactive graphics that can respond to user actions. This change is significant as it enhances the overall web experience, making SVGs more functional and engaging for users across various websites.

features.label: Image tests

features.summary: Recent changes in the image testing area of Firefox involve increasing the fuzz factor for a specific test that checks the downscaling of Mozilla icons. This adjustment is important because it allows for greater tolerance of minor variations that may occur during the image processing, ensuring that the browser displays images more consistently across different platforms and resolutions. While users may not notice a visible change directly, these enhancements contribute to a smoother overall image handling experience in the browser.

features.label: WebGPU backend (wgpu)

features.summary: Recent updates to the WebGPU backend in Firefox bring the browser closer to the latest standards for high-performance graphics and computation on the web. By updating the wgpu library to a more current version, Firefox is enhancing its support for this powerful API, which allows developers to leverage the capabilities of modern GPUs directly from web applications. This change is significant because it paves the way for richer graphics rendering and better overall performance in web applications that utilize GPU resources, improving the experience for users engaging with graphic-intensive content. **In particular, this update helps ensure compatibility with cutting-edge web technologies and features, making Firefox more robust for future web development.**

features.summary: Recent updates in the Firefox browser's compositing and layers area have introduced various enhancements aimed at improving performance and responsiveness during user interactions. Notably, **these changes enhance touch response for web pages, particularly through refinements to the Async Pan/Zoom (APZ) framework**, which helps manage input for smoother scrolling and panning experiences. The updates streamline how touch events are processed, leading to quicker responses during actions like fast flinging and touch movements while also better managing the rendering of high dynamic range (HDR) content on systems utilizing the Wayland display server. Overall, these improvements contribute to more efficient rendering and a smoother experience for users navigating web content.

features.summary: Recent updates to WebRender, the component that enhances Firefox's graphics rendering using the GPU, focus on improving image processing and rendering efficiency. A key highlight is the implementation of **quad shaders for images**, which allows for more advanced visual effects and better handling of various image types, including support for non-premultiplied images and tiled images. Additionally, the normalization of external scrolling offsets and the introduction of an `OffsetFrame` spatial node enhance how content is displayed and scrolled on the screen, providing a smoother experience. These changes not only streamline the rendering pipeline but also improve the overall visual quality of web applications, especially those with complex graphics or media elements.

features.summary: Recent updates to the 2D graphics component of Firefox, known as Thebes, have focused on optimizing font handling and enhancing video processing capabilities. A key improvement is the **introduction of a fast-path in font range calculations**, which should significantly speed up text rendering for users, especially when dealing with large amounts of text. Additionally, the adjustments to hardware video decoding for macOS address important signing constraints, ensuring better security and reliability. The changes also enhance support for multi-GPU systems, which can improve graphics performance in various scenarios, especially in demanding applications or video rendering. These enhancements all contribute to a smoother and more efficient browsing experience.

features.summary: Recent changes to the Canvas area of Firefox primarily enhance the performance and reliability of WebGL, the technology used for rendering interactive 3D graphics. By clamping the maximum number of draw buffers and requiring higher shading language versions for desktop contexts, these updates ensure that developers can leverage more advanced graphics features safely and effectively. Additionally, improvements in memory management and code cleanliness, such as migrating to smart pointers, optimize resource usage while reducing potential memory leaks. This overall refinement in WebGL not only leads to better graphics performance but also makes it easier for developers to create rich visual experiences on the web.  **Overall, these changes help provide a more stable and efficient platform for developers to create immersive graphics applications.**

features.summary: Recent updates to the 2D graphics library in Firefox, known as Moz2D, focused primarily on improving memory management and code efficiency. A significant change is the shift to safer allocation methods like **MakeUnique** and **MakeRefPtr**, which help prevent memory leaks by better managing object lifetimes. Additionally, the code has been streamlined with the replacement of traditional looping constructs with modern approaches like `for_each`, making it cleaner and easier to maintain. These enhancements not only improve the stability and performance of graphic rendering but also ensure that the library is better aligned with newer standards and practices, thus enhancing the overall user experience in graphically intensive web applications.

features.summary: Recent changes in Firefox's image decoding capabilities focus on improving handling of various image formats and enhancing memory management. Notably, the browser now properly decodes **animated GIFs with zero first-frame delay**, which means those animations will play correctly without issues. Additionally, improvements in managing memory for graphical objects, such as using `MakeRefPtr` for resource allocation, help ensure that images are loaded efficiently and reduce the risk of memory leaks. These updates enhance overall performance, reliability, and user experience when viewing images and animations on the web.

features.summary: Recently, Firefox made several improvements to its OpenGL (Open Graphics Library) management, focusing on better compliance with modern coding standards and performance optimizations. These changes include ensuring that unnecessary graphics reset status symbols are not loaded when they aren't needed, which helps streamline the use of system resources. Additionally, the integration of various coding checks through tools like clang-tidy aims to enhance code quality and maintainability. This work is essential for ensuring Firefox runs smoothly and efficiently on devices that leverage OpenGL for graphics rendering, ultimately improving the overall user experience when using graphics-intensive web applications. **This attention to underlying graphics processing is crucial as web applications increasingly rely on high-performance visuals.**

features.summary: Recent updates in the image testing area of Firefox have focused on improving accuracy and compatibility for various image formats. Adjustments were made to the fuzziness tolerances when comparing images to ensure visual consistency, particularly when downscaling icons and dealing with new image formats like JXL (JPEG XL), which offers improved compression and quality. Additionally, support was added for Adobe-style extensions in image headers, enabling better handling of these resources. These enhancements are essential for maintaining display quality across different devices and ensuring that Firefox can properly interpret and process images from various sources, which ultimately contributes to a smoother user experience. **These updates are crucial as they enhance Firefox's capability to handle modern and efficient image formats, thereby improving performance and image fidelity.**

features.label: WebRender bindings

features.summary: Recent updates to the WebRender bindings in Firefox focus on improving video playback and overall rendering performance. One significant change is the ability to handle **HDR (High Dynamic Range) video more effectively**; the browser will now keep video overlays enabled for HDR content even in scenarios where performance might be slower than desired, ensuring a better viewing experience for users watching high-quality content. Additionally, there are improvements to how video stream metadata is processed, particularly for HDR, allowing for better color accuracy and visual fidelity. Other changes involve enhancing error handling during image rendering and optimizing resource management for WebGL gaming, ensuring a smoother experience when using graphics-intensive features. Overall, these updates aim to provide users with a more seamless and visually appealing experience while browsing and viewing media.

features.label: Canvas tests

patches.author: Florian Quèze

features.summary: Recent updates to the Canvas testing infrastructure in Firefox have introduced several important enhancements. One key change is the **split of the generated WebGL mochitest manifest into per-cluster files**, which makes it easier for developers to manage and run tests more effectively, improving the overall testing process. Additionally, the error messages for `createImageBitmap`, a function that helps convert images into usable formats for canvas drawing, have been improved to provide clearer information when something goes wrong. Furthermore, support has been added for the updated Azure A10 WebGL renderer string, ensuring better compatibility and performance for users on systems utilizing this graphics processing technology. These changes collectively aim to enhance the reliability and clarity of the Canvas features in Firefox.

features.label: WebGPU

patches.author: Andy Leiserson
patches.subject: `canvas.getContext("webgpu")` should return null when WebGPU is not supported

patches.author: Andy Leiserson
patches.subject: Disable WebGPU on 32-bit targets

patches.author: Kagami Sascha Rosylight
patches.subject: Part 6: Add missing cycle collection entries in dom/(misc)

features.summary: Recent updates to WebGPU, a technology that enables advanced graphics and computation in web applications, enhance its robustness and manageability. Now, when users attempt to access WebGPU via `canvas.getContext("webgpu")` on unsupported platforms, it will return null—this helps prevent confusion for developers and users alike. Additionally, WebGPU has been disabled on 32-bit systems to ensure optimal performance and security, as the technology is better suited for 64-bit architectures. These changes contribute to a more reliable experience for developers working with graphics and computation in a browser environment, ensuring that they can target features only where they're fully supported. _Overall, users will notice **more consistent behavior** when using WebGPU on capable devices._

features.summary: Recent updates to the WebGPU backend in Firefox enhance its support for this powerful graphics API, which allows web developers to access GPU (graphics processing unit) capabilities directly for improved performance in rendering and computing tasks. By incorporating the latest changes from the upstream version of wgpu, Firefox users will benefit from better compatibility with modern web applications that leverage **WebGPU**, ultimately leading to richer visual experiences and more responsive applications. These updates also help ensure that Firefox remains competitive as more websites utilize advanced graphic techniques and 3D rendering capabilities.

features.label: Graphics tests

patches.author: Lee Salzman
patches.subject: Ensure DrawTargetSkia::CanCreateSimilarDrawTarget checks buffer size.

patches.author: Hiroyuki Ikezoe
patches.subject: Use PuppetWidget for window less browser even on headless mode.

features.summary: Recent updates in the graphics tests area have improved how Firefox handles rendering targets. The change ensures that the **DrawTargetSkia** component can accurately check if it can create similar drawing surfaces based on the buffer size, enhancing performance and efficiency in rendering graphics. Additionally, the use of **PuppetWidget** for handling window-less operations, even in headless mode (where the browser runs without a user interface), allows for more consistent testing behavior. These updates matter as they contribute to better graphics handling and reliability within the browser, ensuring a smoother experience for users dealing with various visual content.

features.label: WebGPU tests

features.summary: Recent updates to the WebGPU Conformance Test Suite (CTS) have been made, ensuring it reflects the latest standards and improvements in WebGPU, a technology that allows web applications to leverage the power of GPU for rendering graphics and running computations. These updates are essential for validating the implementation of WebGPU across different browsers, as they help developers ensure that their use of this API will perform consistently and correctly. This is particularly important as the web continues to evolve towards more visually rich and interactive experiences, making reliable performance in graphics processing a priority.

features.label: Graphics configuration

patches.author: Alastor Wu
patches.subject: Part 1: Add PDMFactorySupport singleton with pref/gfxVar invalidation.

features.summary: Recent changes in the graphics configuration of Firefox introduced a new component called **PDMFactorySupport**, which manages preferences and graphics variables more effectively. This enhancement allows the browser to better handle different types of media processing and support, ensuring that graphics rendering is optimized according to user settings or hardware capabilities. Such improvements are crucial for a smoother browsing experience, particularly for media-heavy tasks like video playback and gaming, _though users might not notice immediate visual differences_.

features.label: Graphics core

patches.author: Bas Schouten
patches.subject: Remove legacy mirrors for a large set of probes primarily used for performance monitoring.

features.summary: Recent updates to the graphics core of Firefox involved the **removal of legacy mirrors for a large set of performance monitoring probes**. These probes are tools used to gather data on how well the browser performs, specifically in rendering graphics. By eliminating outdated components, the Firefox team aims to streamline performance monitoring and focus on more modern, efficient methods, ultimately leading to better optimization and resource use in the browser's graphics handling.

features.label: Image decoders

patches.author: Valentin Gosu
patches.subject: Make nsIURI.rs usable in rust

features.summary: Recent updates to the image decoders in Firefox focused on enhancing compatibility for developers by making *nsIURI.rs*, a representation of URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers) in Rust, more usable. This change is significant because it allows for better integration with modern web technologies, enabling smoother handling of image resources. With the growing importance of web performance and efficient resource management, these improvements facilitate the development of faster and more reliable web applications. While this change will primarily benefit developers, users can expect a more seamless browsing experience as a result.

features.label: Video colour conversion (YCbCr)

patches.author: serge-sans-paille
patches.subject: Tell the build system that some assembly files need preprocessing

features.summary: Recent updates to the video colour conversion process in Firefox focused on enhancing the handling of assembly files—specifically, informing the build system that certain files require preprocessing. This change is a technical step that ensures the efficient conversion of video formats, which is crucial for maintaining colour accuracy and quality when streaming content. While users may not see a direct change, this improvement lays the groundwork for better performance and support for advanced video features in the future.
